document.title: eco Guide for LLMs
document.purpose: Guide LLM instances in understanding eco, running ecologies, authoring ecology directories, configuring ecology.json, and keeping the system operational.

orientation.short-version: eco copies an executable directory tree into a timestamped run directory, runs the copied version, and records what happened.
mental-model: An ecology is a source directory that knows how to run itself.
The source ecology is a template.
The run directory is the working copy.
eco executes only inside the copied run directory.
The source ecology should not be mutated by execution.
Each run leaves behind inspectable filesystem evidence.
why-it-exists: To make program runs reproducible and inspectable.
To keep source directories clean while still allowing programs to write outputs.
To preserve the exact executable directory tree used for a run.
To provide a tiny substrate that richer tools can build on without making eco itself into a workflow engine.

ecology.definition: A source directory tree containing ecology.json and any files needed by the program being run.
notes: The ecology may contain Python modules, scripts, assets, prompts, JSON files, shell scripts, data files, or arbitrary other files.
eco treats the ecology as opaque except for ecology.json.
The ecology should be authored so that it can run from a copied directory.

ecology-json.definition: The control file inside an ecology that tells eco how to execute the copied ecology.

run-directory.definition: A timestamped directory created under execpath.rundir that contains a copy of the ecology and execution metadata.

eco-run-json.definition: Metadata written by eco into the run directory before and after execution.

what-eco-does: Loads ecology.json from the source ecology directory.
Validates the small set of fields eco understands.
Allocates a fresh run directory under execpath.rundir.
Deep-copies the entire ecology directory into the run directory.
Runs the declared subprocess from inside the copied directory.
Rewrites ECO-RUN.json with status COMPLETE or FAILED.
When times is greater than 1, repeats the copy-run-record cycle in series and stops at the first failure.
what-eco-does-not-do: eco does not define workflow semantics.
eco does not define graph execution.
eco does not schedule delayed or background runs.
eco does not interpret program outputs.
eco does not understand the domain meaning of an ecology.
eco does not mutate the source ecology during execution.

run.purpose: Copy the configured ecology into a new run directory and execute it.

set.purpose: Built-in lionscliapp command for persisting configuration values.

get.purpose: Built-in lionscliapp command for inspecting a configuration value.

keys.purpose: Built-in lionscliapp command for listing declared configuration keys.

help.purpose: Built-in lionscliapp command for help text.

execpath.ecology.purpose: Path to the source ecology directory containing ecology.json.
notes: lionscliapp expands and resolves this value before eco uses it.
Relative paths are resolved against the execution root.

execpath.rundir.purpose: Path to the parent directory where eco creates run directories.
notes: The directory is created if it does not exist.
Do not place execpath.rundir inside the source ecology.

times.purpose: Number of runs to perform in series.
rules: Each run receives its own run directory.
If a run fails, eco stops the series and does not start the remaining runs.

common-invocations.purpose: Run an ecology with all paths provided for this invocation.
Run the same ecology several times in series.

ecology-json.purpose: Describe how eco should execute the copied ecology.

identity.name: hello-ecology
identity.description: Tiny ecology used to demonstrate eco.

eco-version.meaning: Version of the ecology file format understood by eco.

identity.meaning: Optional human-readable description of the ecology.

keys.name: Short name for the ecology.
keys.description: Plain-language explanation of what this ecology is for.

execution.entry-type.meaning: Execution mechanism. eco v1 supports subprocess only.

execution.cwd.meaning: Working directory relative to the copied run directory.
rules: Must stay inside the copied run directory.
Must exist after the ecology has been copied.

execution.command.meaning: Command list passed directly to subprocess.run().
rules: Use list form, not a single shell string.
Each argument is one list item.
Prefer commands that work from the declared cwd.

execution.environment.meaning: Environment variables added to the subprocess environment.
rules: These values supplement the normal process environment.

checklist: Create a directory for the ecology.
Place the program, scripts, modules, data, assets, and support files inside it.
Make sure the program writes outputs relative to its working directory, not into hard-coded source paths.
Create ecology.json at the top of the ecology directory.
Set execution.cwd to the directory where the command should run after copying.
Set execution.command as an array of strings.
Optionally set execution.environment for run-specific environment variables.
Run eco against the ecology and inspect the created run directory.
good-practices: Use relative file paths inside ecology programs whenever practical.
Write outputs into the current working directory or a relative output directory.
Do not assume the ecology will run from its original source location.
Avoid absolute paths inside ecology.json unless the command intentionally calls a system tool.
Put domain-specific meaning in the ecology's own files, not in eco.

preflight: Confirm the source ecology directory exists.
Confirm ecology.json exists at the ecology root.
Confirm execution.cwd exists relative to the ecology root.
Confirm the command can run on the target machine.
Confirm execpath.rundir is outside the ecology directory.
If using times, confirm it is a positive integer.
If using python, confirm required packages are installed in the environment that runs eco.
steps: Run eco with execpath.ecology and execpath.rundir.
Optionally include --times N to perform N runs in series.
Observe the printed run id and run directory path for each started run.
Open the run directory or directories.
Inspect copied files and generated outputs.
Inspect ECO-RUN.json for status and return code.
expected-success-signs: One new yyyy-mm-dd_run-#### directory appears under execpath.rundir for each completed run.
Each directory contains a copy of the ecology.
The expected program outputs are inside the run directories, not the source ecology.
expected-failure-signs: An error field may appear when eco itself encountered an execution error.
The failed run directory still remains available for inspection.
If times was greater than 1, no later runs are started after the failure.

operational-rules-for-llms: When asked to make an ecology, create the ecology directory and ecology.json together.
When asked to run an ecology, prefer invoking eco rather than manually copying and running unless debugging eco itself.
Use execpath.ecology and execpath.rundir, not bare ecology or rundir.
Use times for serial repetition; do not invent a separate repeat field.
Rely on lionscliapp path coercion for execpath.* values.
Never place the run directory inside the source ecology.
Do not make eco into a workflow engine when adding features.
Do not teach eco domain-specific meanings for ecology contents.
Keep validation at the boundary; after ecology.json is accepted, operational code may trust the normalized structure.
Prefer JSON files for configuration and metadata.
Prefer simple procedural Python over class-heavy architecture in eco itself.

common-mistakes.mistake: Writing execution.command as a single string.
common-mistakes.reason: eco passes the command directly to subprocess.run(); list form is portable and unambiguous.
execution.cwd must be relative to the copied run directory.

common-mistakes.mistake: Writing outputs back into the source ecology.
common-mistakes.reason: eco's purpose is to preserve source ecologies as templates and put run outputs in run directories.
The run directory would then be copied into future runs, causing recursive growth and confusing artifacts.

common-mistakes.mistake: Setting times to zero, a negative number, or a non-integer string.
common-mistakes.reason: times must be a positive integer because eco performs a concrete sequence of one or more runs.

common-mistakes.mistake: Assuming eco understands the ecology's domain.
common-mistakes.reason: eco only understands execution mechanics. Meaning belongs to the ecology or higher-level tools.

if-eco-cannot-find-ecology-json: Check that execpath.ecology points to the ecology root, not a parent directory.
Check the file is named exactly ecology.json.
Use eco get execpath.ecology to inspect the resolved path when persisted configuration is involved.
if-the-command-fails: Open the run directory and inspect ECO-RUN.json.
Run the command manually from the run directory using the same cwd.
Check whether required Python packages are installed.
Check whether the command depends on files that were not included in the ecology.
Check whether the program used source-specific absolute paths.
if-outputs-appear-in-the-source-ecology: Inspect the ecology program for absolute paths.
Change output paths to be relative to execution.cwd.
Rerun eco and confirm outputs appear in the run directory.
if-run-directories-grow-recursively: Move existing run directories out of the ecology source tree.
Rerun with a clean external rundir.

lionscliapp: eco is implemented as a lionscliapp application.
lionscliapp provides command dispatch, persistent configuration, built-in set/get/help/keys commands, and execpath.* path coercion.
LLMs should preserve the execpath.* convention when modifying eco.
filetalk: eco is compatible with filesystem-oriented systems such as FileTalk because runs leave structured files behind.
eco is not itself an IPC system.
battery-runner: battery-runner can be placed inside an ecology or launched by an ecology command if desired.
eco should still only copy and execute; ongoing orchestration belongs to the program being run.
workflow-systems: A workflow system may call eco to create isolated run artifacts.
The workflow system should own workflow semantics; eco should remain the crystallization runner.

future-extension-guidance: Prefer adding small fields only when they support execution crystallization.
Do not add graph, pipeline, or scheduling semantics directly to eco v1.
If richer orchestration appears necessary, build it as a layer above eco.
Preserve backward compatibility with ecology.json eco-version 1.0 unless deliberately creating a new version.
Document new ecology.json fields in docs/raw before depending on them in code.

minimal-test-ecology.success-condition: A new run directory contains ecology.json, run.py, hello-output.txt, and ECO-RUN.json with status COMPLETE.
